analysis: Stronger solutions converge on a common pattern: prune irrelevant candidates, build one shared approximation of the dominated region (Monte‑Carlo samples or boxed volumes), reuse that structure (boolean masks) and perform a lazy‑greedy selection (max‑heap) with incremental overlap subtraction, then spend a tiny budget on exact polishing (shortlist swaps using pygmo). Weaker ones tend to re‑compute exact hypervolume many times inside inner loops (per candidate or per remove/insert), recreate heavy objects repeatedly, or lack data reuse (no shared samples/masks). Memory‑safe block construction of masks and vectorized overlap updates (seen in heuristics 3–6, 8) give excellent speed/quality tradeoffs. Avoidable overheads include repeated pg.hypervolume object creation, full re‑scans instead of lazy updates, and large unbounded polish searches; targeted evolutionary ops (heuristic 2) can find good sets but pay heavy evaluation cost and bookkeeping overhead.
experience: Prune dominated points, build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, use a lazy greedy heap with incremental overlap updates, then polish only a small shortlist with exact HV. Cache hypervolume objects, scale sample count with k and D, and cap refinement iterations. These give best quality per runtime.
current_reflection: Prune dominated points, build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, use a lazy greedy heap with incremental overlap updates, then polish only a small shortlist with exact HV. Cache hypervolume objects, scale sample count with k and D, and cap refinement iterations. These give best quality per runtime.
bad_reflections: Favor: nondominated pruning + one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, lazy greedy selection (heap) with incremental overlap subtraction, and a tiny exact‑HV swap on a short shortlist. Cap memory, limit swap budget, reuse vectors/masks, and avoid repeated exact HV calls inside inner loops.

Prune dominated points early, reuse one MC sample set and block‑computed boolean masks, and drive selection with a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains (incrementally subtract overlaps). Limit exact hypervolume calls to a small shortlisted set and cap polish iterations. Vectorize updates, avoid per‑candidate full HV recomputations, and use box‑volume tie‑breakers and deterministic seeds for stable, fast heuristics.

Prune dominated/ref‑worse points, use one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean masks, run lazy greedy via a max‑heap with incremental overlap updates, tie‑break by cheap box‑volumes, and do a bounded exact polish on a small shortlist. Cap samples/iterations, vectorize inner work, reuse hypervolume objects, and seed RNGs for reproducibility.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: dominated pruning, shared MC samples, boolean masks, lazy greedy heap, incremental overlap, exact-HV polish, caching, block computation, deterministic seed
- Advice: prune dominated/ref‑worse points; build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks and precompute boolean domination masks; run a lazy max‑heap with incremental overlap subtraction and cheap box‑volume tie‑breakers; shortlist a few candidates and run a bounded exact‑HV polish; cache hypervolume objects, reuse vectors/masks, scale sample count with k and D, and cap refinement iterations.
- Avoid: per‑candidate full HV recomputations, unbounded polish loops, keeping huge MC arrays in memory, and unseeded randomness.
- Explanation: approximate screening + incremental updates concentrate costly exact HV work on a tiny shortlist, yielding best quality per runtime and predictable memory use.
